
::: {.paper-table}
**Table 25. B.7 Policy and behavioral architecture — changing the default**

| Intervention | Outcome | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Auto-enrollment in 401(k) | Participation | 37% → 86% (Madrian & Shea [@madrian2001] ); persists long-term | Multi-cohort evidence |
| Opt-out organ donation | Registered donors | ~15% (opt-in countries) → 85–99% (opt-out) | Johnson & Goldstein [@johnson2003] |
| EITC expansions | Single-mother labor supply | Large positive employment effects | Eissa & Liebman; Meyer & Rosenbaum |
| Conditional cash transfers (Progresa/Oportunidades) | School attendance, health visits | Large and durable | Schultz; Gertler |
| Large unconditional cash transfers (GiveDirectly) | Consumption, assets | Durable increases in assets and consumption | Haushofer & Shapiro; Egger et al. |
| Graduation programs for ultra-poor | Consumption, assets, mental health | Durable effects at 3 and 7 years across 6 countries | Banerjee et al. [@banerjee2015] Science , Bandiera et al. |
| Smoking bans in public places | Acute coronary events | ~15% reduction in hospital admissions | Multiple pre/post studies, IOM review |
| Graduated licensing | Teen crash mortality | ~20–40% reduction | CDC systematic reviews |
| Seatbelt laws + primary enforcement | Motor vehicle fatalities | Substantial and durable | NHTSA |
| Motorcycle helmet laws | Motorcyclist mortality | ~37% reduction in fatal injury risk | Cochrane |
| Alcohol taxation | Consumption, harm | Elasticity ~−0.5; translates to substantial mortality effects | Wagenaar meta-analyses |
:::
